The new plausibility checks feature in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management is a powerful tool designed to verify the validity and consistency of footprint calculations, ensuring data integrity and reliability. This feature decreases the necessity for manual verification, which can be impractical or time-consuming. It ensures that values entered or calculated fall within a specified tolerance, thereby reducing errors. Pre-shipped most common templates support a quick time to value. Through customizing those templates to your specific business needs, you can identify deviations or anomalies in your data and take appropriate corrective actions.

The Manage Plausibility Checks app allows you to set up and manage plausibility checks for calculated footprints, offering a quick overview of deviations from expected results to identify anomalies. After footprint calculations are completed in the Calculate Footprints app, plausibility checks are automatically triggered, and results can be evaluated in the Monitor Plausibility Checks app. Note that plausibility checks on spend-based calculations are currently not supported.

Within the Manage Plausibility Checks app, you can configure checks, customize them, or use SAP-provided ones, assign checks to multiple results or multiple checks to a single item, and edit, delete, or copy custom checks. The process for creating plausibility checks involves setting acceptable deviation thresholds for calculating total CO2e emissions, CO2e emission per unit, or product quantity for various item types. You need to enter an ID and description, and select the check type, either Absolute (with maximum and minimum thresholds) or Relative (based on variation from the previous period). SAP provides default relative checks that can be edited but not deleted or copied. You specify validity dates and details regarding item types, such as footprint inventory scope and plant. Depending on the selected item type, additional fields may appear. For absolute checks, you define maximum and minimum values and units of measure. For relative checks, enter an acceptable percentage variance from the previous period. Once created and released, the plausibility checks are ready for use on calculated footprints. To manage plausibility checks, ensure you have the required roles assigned. For more information, check Manage Plausibility Checks on SAP Help.

Manage Plausibility Checks

In the Calculate Footprints app, the released plausibility checks are automatically triggered during the footprint calculation run. The status Calculated with Warnings indicates that the calculation doesn't adhere to the defined checks. A new micro chart also summarizes the footprint status, representing the number of successful calculations in green, those with warnings in orange, and those with errors in red. To view the details, click on the new Show Plausibility Checks button.

The Monitor Plausibility Checks app allows you to evaluate the results of plausibility checks conducted on footprint inventory calculations. It displays warning messages if any of the defined and released checks are violated, enabling analysis of their nature and extent. The app also lets you add review comments to the results for future reporting or auditing. You can select the appropriate alert that appears if the calculations deviate from the configured checks to see all details. To confirm your review, select the Review action and add any necessary comments, particularly if the values deviate due to critical operational situations. This will mark the check as Reviewed. If you retrigger calculations and the calculated value remains unchanged with the associated check still in place, your previous comments will be retained. To use this app, ensure you have the correct roles assigned. See Monitor Plausibility Checks on SAP Help for more information.

Monitor Plausibility Checks

Furthermore, the Manage Footprint Results app now displays the warning status for item footprint results. By navigating to the Errors and Warnings tab, you can get more details about the plausibility warnings, ensuring a thorough analysis of any issues. In addition, the Monitor Business Logs app provides traceability by allowing you to trace back who deleted plausibility checks at a specific point in time, ensuring accountability and traceability.

 

Interplant Production

You can now calculate the CO2e emissions associated with Goods Issued to Production and Goods Receipt from Production, even when the production and movement plants are different. We've enhanced the Import Business Transactions app by adding new consistency checks. These checks ensure that any production documents referenced in business transactions exist within the master data and that the associated plant is included in the defined footprint inventory scope. See Import Business Transactions for more information.

As an example, your company utilizes two distinct plants for efficient production processes: Plant A (the movement plant), where goods are sourced, and Plant B (the production plant), where goods are manufactured. When setting up the production workflow in your systems, use the Import Master Data app to link production documents to Plant B, indicating where production occurs. Meanwhile, the Import Business Transactions app is used to document the flow of goods, noting that goods for production originate from Plant A. To ensure seamless data integration and consistency, both plants are included in the Manage Footprint Inventory Scopes app. This setup allows to effectively connect production activities at Plant B with incoming goods from Plant A, streamlining the overall production operations.

 

Other Release Highlights

With the new Emission Factors OData API, you can retrieve emission factors that were imported through the Manage Emission Factors app. API allows you to export emission factors data for analytics or reporting purposes. For more information, check the API reference SAP Business Accelerator Hub: Emission Factors.

Considering the effect of fiscal periods on the preparation and publication of product footprints for carbon data exchange, the latest update to the Publish Product Footprints app enhances how product quantities and total CO2e emissions are calculated. By calculating weighted values based on the number of overlapping days between the fiscal period and the average footprint periodicity specified in the app, a more precise and accurate footprint calculation is provided. This approach allows for a linear estimation of carbon emissions over the given period. For examples and detailed information, see Preparing and Publishing Product Footprints.

With a UI5 enhancement, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Fiori applications now allow users to save public variants. This new feature lets you decide if your saved variants are visible just to you or accessible to all users. Note that to leverage UI5 flexibility services as key users, specific roles are required – see Setting Up Roles for more information.

Instead of endless periods, the Manage Purchased Product Footprints app now suggests a three-year validity period when releasing a purchased product footprint or manually mapping emission factors. You can adjust these dates as needed. Furthermore, you can enhance your data processing efficiency by filtering records using the Valid On field across all tabs. For more information, see Using AI to Map Purchased Products and Purchased Product Groups and Manually Map Purchased Products and Product Groups without Footprints.

 

Integrations with SAP Green Ledger and SAP Spend Control Tower

In December, SAP Green Ledger went general available (Read the press release: SAP Announces General Availability of SAP Green Ledger). This innovative carbon accounting solution is integrated with SAP's financial management system running on SAP S/4HANA Cloud. It’s designed to help businesses seamlessly track, measure, and report their carbon emissions alongside financial data at a transactional level, enabling a holistic view of both environmental and financial performance. SAP Green Ledger is a tool that integrates carbon emissions data with financial accounting, enabling businesses to forecast, budget, and contextualize carbon and financial data together for informed decision-making and strategic planning. It analyzes Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions and aligns them with financial data to account for all sources of emissions. By applying double-entry accounting principles, SAP Green Ledger ensures reliable and auditable carbon data, helping businesses align profitability with sustainability goals. It enables carbon budgeting and benchmarking across business units to drive low-carbon strategies and regulatory compliance.

SAP Green Ledger is also integrated with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, enhancing the reporting and analysis capabilities and providing a more comprehensive view of carbon data. To import your calculated corporate carbon footprints from SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, you need to make those available via the Manage Footprint Communications app. An internal API provides those footprints at the level of business transaction, period, GHG scope and GHG category. SAP Green Ledger uses this internal API for replicating the corporate footprints. This data is then posted to the journal at the entity level, ensuring accurate and detailed carbon accounting. See Integrating with SAP Green Ledger on SAP Help for all required steps in both systems.

For more general product information, check the product page: SAP Green Ledger. For even more insights and a system demo, I also recommend watching the recording of the following webinar:

Another exciting product that was launched last year is SAP Spend Control Tower. This next-generation spend analytics solution is designed to provide comprehensive visibility into enterprise-wide spend data. It integrates payment and supplier data from various sources into a single, unified view. Key features include AI-enabled spend classification, which uses machine learning to optimize spend categories and supplier data, and preconfigured dashboards that offer intuitive views with key performance indicators (KPIs) for spend performance. Additionally, it includes supplier risk management to identify sourcing vulnerabilities and minimize supplier risks, as well as diversity and sustainability tracking to monitor and qualify suppliers based on these goals. Check the product page for more details: SAP Spend Control Tower.

SAP Spend Control Tower integrates with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, enhancing its capabilities to provide a holistic view of both financial and environmental impacts. This integration allows businesses to track and analyze their carbon footprint alongside spend data, offering insights into the sustainability performance of their procurement activities. By combining spend analytics with sustainability metrics, companies can make more informed decisions that align with their environmental goals. For this integration, the standard Public APIs are leveraged. See Setting Up Connection with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management on SAP Help for all details and required steps.

SAP Spend Control Tower

 

Outlook for 2025

For 2025, there are a bunch of exciting innovations on which we’re currently working and that we plan to release in SAP Sustainability Footprint Management during this year. In the following, I’ll go into some of the feature highlights planned for release in the first half-year. For a comprehensive overview of all planned innovations and which benefits those will bring to you, have a look into SAP Road Map Explorer, which will be updated regularly.

The emission factor mass update enables an automatic updating of existing emissions factor references in business entities when importing a new emissions factor or lifecycle assessment (LCA) content package. This ensures you have always mapped your business entities with the latest emissions factors, reducing the need for manual updates and efforts.

Through enabling internal energy production with multiple energy carriers, you can model the production of energy in the energy flow model. By enhancing process infrastructure to accommodate multiple energy carriers with a unified unit of measure (UoM) dimension, it ensures precise footprint calculations that account for internal energy productions such as electricity and heat.

We further plan to provide an interface to extract organizational footprint inventories on transactional level, allowing you to retrieve data filtered by inventory scope, item type, and various periods. It includes measures like calculated carbon equivalents and business activity quantities, with dimensions varying according to the footprint item type. This will facilitate custom analytical and reporting applications, enhancing the solution's extensibility.

With the product footprint item fact sheet, we plan to offer a comprehensive, itemized view of all input items contributing to a product's environmental footprint, encompassing energy sources, business activities, purchased products, and transport. By providing specific data points for each line item, it ensures transparency and will help you make informed decisions about the product's sustainability impact.

To improve the accuracy in calculating freight transport emissions for both upstream (Scope 3.4) and downstream (Scope 3.9) activities, we’re enhancing freight calculation by using ERP material flows. It automates route determination and integrates freight calculation results into your organization's footprint inventory, helping you to manage transportation operations and emission data with higher precision and less manual effort. An analytical app is planned for detailed transport calculations, improving overall efficiency in managing and reducing transport footprints.

For ensuring precise and flexible financial tracking over different time periods and for various location types, cost center assignments for energy sources and locations improve the accuracy of carbon accounting and seamless integration with SAP Green Ledger.

Finally, in the first half of this year we also plan to enable the data collection definition via location types. This allows you to define business activities based on various location types, such as plants, facilities, and resources, enabling the generation of specialized GHG data collection forms. By tailoring forms to specific location types and activities, the feature simplifies and streamlines the process of data collection, ensuring more accurate and relevant environmental reporting.

 

Customer Success Story: BlueTriton

In its pursuit of enhancing the quality of water sources and delivering healthy hydration, BlueTriton Brands Inc., North America's leading branded water company, has embarked on a transformative journey with SAP Sustainability solutions. Recognizing that their business goes beyond just water consumption – spanning from source to sip – BlueTriton is dedicated to conserving resources, stewarding nature, and advancing circular solutions.

To meet robust sustainability targets, BlueTriton integrated SAP solutions to effectively track their environmental impact. These solutions, including SAP Sustainability Control Tower and SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, enable the company to manage greenhouse gas emissions, track ESG goals, and plan sustainable initiatives. The SAP S/4HANA Cloud solution for EHS environment management further bolsters ESG reporting, while SAP Responsible Design and Production aids in designing sustainable products.

With SAP Sustainability solutions, BlueTriton now accurately measures its carbon footprint at a site level, allowing for a detailed comparison of emissions across individual factories against corporate targets. This capability has enabled pinpointing of issues and strategic action to ensure each site meets its carbon reduction goals. With granular insights and precise reporting, BlueTriton not only supports informed decision-making but also fulfills anticipated compliance requirements in California. The increased transparency and accountability in its sustainability efforts allow the company to confidently share its progress with stakeholders. By effectively quantifying and analyzing its environmental performance, BlueTriton aims to build awareness around climate-related risks and opportunities, ultimately reinforcing its reputation as a trusted guardian of water resources and enhancing customer loyalty. Looking forward, BlueTriton plans to leverage AI-driven insights to assess future scenarios, helping refine sustainable strategies in alignment with shifting temperatures, consumer preferences, and economic factors.

Read the full story here BlueTriton: Achieving healthy hydration through water stewardship and have a look into the video: Achieving healthy hydration through water stewardship.

 

New Tools to Discover and Master Corporate Carbon Footprint Calculation

In the last quarter, we introduced new assets and enhanced existing ones, aiming to support you in getting to know and adopting SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. Those assets are all free of charge and specifically designed to guide you in calculating, managing, and reporting your Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF).

We released the new Learning Journey: Establishing Corporate Carbon Footprint Emissions with Simple Configuration. This two-hour learning targets beginners and provides a great introduction into the Greenhouse Gas Protocol and into the general capabilities and architecture of SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. It shows how you can configure the solution and use business activities for calculating your CCF across GHG Scope 1, 2, and 3. It also covers the integration of calculated footprints into SAP Sustainability Control Tower. After successful completion of the course and passing all quizzes, you receive a Record of Achievement and digital badge. Start your learning now for free: Establishing Corporate Carbon Footprint Emissions with Simple Configuration.

Learning Journey: Establishing Corporate Carbon Footprint Emissions with Simple Configuration

Besides this course for beginners, we’re also working on an intermediate Learning Journey, guiding you through the calculation of selected GHG Scopes by using advanced configuration. You’ll learn how you can best leverage the ERP-centric approach of SAP Sustainability Footprint Management to re-use your business data in calculating emissions related to facilities, products, waste, and more. The release is expected soon, stay tuned!

We’ve also revamped and enhanced the Free Trial access for SAP Sustainability Footprint Management. This free-of-charge 30-day trial offers a hands-on experience with the live system, perfect for testing and exploring its features. The trial already showcased crucial elements for Product Carbon Footprint management, and now we've enriched it by introducing a second model company for Corporate Carbon Footprint calculations. With two model companies – Almika Soft (software) and Almika Foods (bakery) – you can delve into various carbon footprint scenarios seamlessly. Additionally, our revamped start page now makes accessing guided tours easier, organized by topics and three levels of detail, allowing you to select either a quick overview or a more in-depth exploration. Register now for free and receive instant access information via e-mail: SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Trial.

SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Free Trial (Landing Page)

Finally, also the documentation on SAP Help has been enhanced. A new and improved Scenario Guide provides configuration and usage guidance for calculating your corporate carbon footprint, supported by step-by-step processes and examples. Explore the guide: Scenario Guide for SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, note that this is available in English only.

Available for some time already but still worth mentioning again, our Implementation Guide (for Partners) provides a detailed guidance on setting up the solution for calculating corporate carbon footprints. This package includes sample data for a model company and an implementation book to do an end-to-end setup of SAP Sustainability Footprint Management for calculating the CCF of the example company. You can download the material here: Corporate Carbon Footprint (CCF) Implementation Guide. Note that the target audience is consultants and partners, and access is restricted, requiring a login via your SAP account.

 

Additional Information

For in-depth details on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, including concepts, setup, and the application help, I recommend you browsing through the SAP Help Portal. For an overview of all new features and functions that have been released, check the What’s New section, which is updated with every new software release.

To stay up to date on SAP Sustainability Footprint Management, follow me here in the SAP Community. In regular cadence, I’ll publish blog posts giving you updates on all the product’s new features and functions as well as deep dives into the product capabilities. I’m also looking forward to engaging with you directly, feel free to leave your feedback in the comment section.

entry.title: Celebrating the Latest Public Cloud Release for Product Compliance
entry.content:
Today marks an exciting milestone with the latest Public Cloud release for Product Compliance. This update brings new features, updated content, and improved processes, all designed to enhance user experience and streamline compliance management.

For those dealing with dangerous goods, staying compliant is crucial. The latest release includes new compliance requirement versions for the Australian Dangerous Goods Code (ADG) and the International Air Transport Association Dangerous Goods Regulations (IATA-DGR). For detailed instructions on connecting to our Regulatory Content Service, see the SAP Note 2999140. Another note on dangerous goods content, there is also a help page dedicated to all things content here.  A side note about the content service, it can be set up to deliver new compliance requirement versions, but you still need to activate the new version for it to be used in the system.

Packing instructions according to dangerous goods regulations data is also visible in the View Regulatory Data - Dangerous Goods app. Packing instructions according to dangerous goods regulation data shows the acceptable single and combination packagings. For combination packagings, the acceptable inner packagings, outer packagings and when applicable, the maximum permitted quantity in each inner or outer packaging are shown per packing group. This information is also part of the Dangerous Goods List for Packing Instructions.

  • For ADR, packing instructions for the following types of packages have been added:
    • Packages other than IBCs: P001, P002, P003, P004, P004, P005, P006, P099, P206, P207, P401, P402, P403, P404, P410, P502, P503, P907, P908, P909, P911
    • Intermediate bulk containers: IBC520, IBC620, IBC99
    • Large Packaging: LP02, LP03, LP101, LP102, LP200, LP 621, LP622, LP902, LP903, LP904, LP905, LP906, LP99

 

Here you can see the View Regulatory Data – Dangerous Goods app, and now there is a clickable link within the Enclosure Instruction Code. This link takes you to additional packaging information, structured like the actual dangerous goods regulations.

This is regulation dependent, and while this is not available for regulations, it is something we will continue to expand as data becomes available.

Moving on to product marketability, there are a lot of updated compliance requirement versions, and you can see complete list with additional information here. You can also see a complete list of available compliance requirement for product marketability here.

  • AU AIIC Status (Jul 2024)
  • CA DSL+NDSL Status (Jun 2024)
  • CA Drug Precursors (Jun 2024)
  • CH Chemical Risk Reduction Ordinance Status (Jun 2024)
  • CN RoHS (Jun 2024)
  • CN IECSC Status (May 2024)
  • EU Drug Precursors (May 2024)
  • EU ODS Status (Feb 2024)
  • EU REACH SVHC Status (Jun 2024)
  • EU Drug Precursors (May 2024)
  • EU REACH Annex XVII Status (May 2024)
  • JP ENCS Status (Jun 2024)
  • JP PDSCL Status (May 2024)
  • JP ISHA Status (Mar 2024 Rev.1)
  • JP CSCL Class 1 and 2 Status (Jul 2024)
  • JP CSCL Exempt Status (Jun 2024)
  • JP CSCL General Chemicals (Jul 2024)
  • MY Poisons List (May 2024)
  • NL ZZS List Status (May 2024)
  • RS Banned + Restricted Substances Status (Apr 2024)
  • SG Drug Precursors (May 2024)
  • US TSCA Export Notification (Jun 2024)
  • US TSCA Inventory Status (May 2024)
  • US TSCA SNUR Status (Jun 2024)
  • World IEC 62474 DSL (Jan 2024)
  • World PFAS Screening List Status (Apr 2024)

For safety data sheet management, authoring and occupational exposure limits there are 2 new compliance requirements and 14 new versions. The complete list with additional info can be found here. You can also see a complete list of available compliance requirement for SDS Management here,  SDS Authoring here, and Occupational Exposure Limits here. 

New compliance requirements

  • EU Specific Aerosol Labelling (Nov 2016)
  • EU Waste Code (Dec 2014)

New Versions

  • Bulgaria. Occupational Exposure Limits (Apr 2024)
  • Canada. Occupational Exposure Limits - British Columbia (Apr 2024)
  • China. Occupational Exposure Limits (Jul 2024)
  • Estonia. Occupational Exposure Limits (Apr 2024)
  • Germany. TRGS Occupational Exposure limits (Jun 2024)
  • Korea. Permissible Exposure Levels (Jun 2024)
  • Latvia. Occupational Exposure Limits (Apr 2024)
  • Netherlands. Occupational Exposure Limits (May 2024)
  • Poland. Occupational Exposure Limits (Jul 2024)
  • Romania. Occupational Exposure Limits (Mar 2024)
  • Singapore. Occupational Exposure Limits (May 2024)
  • Slovakia. Occupational Exposure Limits (Jun 2024)
  • Slovenia. Occupational Exposure Limits (Apr 2024)
  • USA. Workplace Environmental Exposure Levels (WEEL) (Jun 2024)

Finally, don't miss out on the SAP EHS and SAP Product Compliance & Stewardship communities in-person in Budapest, where we’ll be exploring the latest SAP S/4HANA innovations. Whether you are planning for future ERP projects, interested in a training opportunity for a member of your team,  or are looking to learn directly from other users, there won’t be another opportunity in 2025 to meet the full SAP ecosystem face-to-face. Click on the banner for more information.

entry.title: Basics of sustainability solution SAP Product and REACH Compliance
entry.content:
Introduction

The present blog

This blog will give you a first overview to the functionality of SAP Product and REACH Compliance. This solution is available on SAP ECC as AddOn or technically embedded in SAP S/4HANA. Here you get a first flavor of the scope of the solution.

The author

I have been working since years in the area of sustainability supporting customers in implementing product compliance solutions in several industries.

Overview to previously written blogs:

2022: Reference customers and/or customers success stories for SAP Product and REACH Compliance 

2021: How to uninstall SAP Product and REACH Compliance 2.0 (to get ready for SAP S/4HANA) 

2020: Are you affected by SCIP database of ECHA? Think about SAP Product Compliance for Discrete Ind... 

 

SAP Product and REACH Compliance

Customers

In general, all companies that produce discrete items (articles) might benefit from this solution. Automotive customers can interface the <<International Material Data System>> (IMDS) for downloading material data sheets (MDS) from their suppliers and uploading MDS to their customers.

My customers, where I have implemented SAP Product and REACH Compliance, are active in the following areas: 

  • Automotive (both OEM and Tier-1)
  • Manufacturing (flood control, pump, seal, valve, energy)
  • Hot stamping and coating technology (scope is both process and discrete)
  • Aerospace and Defense
  • Medical Engineering
  • Life Science 

Scope overview to SAP Product and REACH Compliance

The figure below introduces the scope of SAP Product and REACH Compliance. 

Regulatory List Management

Regulatory lists are mainly negative substance lists that might contain listed substances or listed substance groups. A listed substance group is for example "lead" that is being used for <<Restriction of Hazardous Substances>> (ROHS) and that contains more than 700 hundred single listed substances. Negative substance list means that the substances are either prohibited or declarable (or even both). Furthermore, the regulatory list defines for each listed substance the threshold and the algorithm for calculation.

Compliance Checks (in Compliance Requirements)

Most of the Compliance Requirements such as ROHS, IMDS, REACH-SVHC*, etc. are linked with exact one regulatory list and consume the data of them to check whether a basic material, component, product, contains these substances above threshold. This check is embedded in the compliance checks where several check criteria are in that ensure that the <<Compliance Data Object>> (CDO) is compliant or not. These compliance requirements might be linked with one default normalization that is applied for all calculations (even in automatic processing)

* REACH-SVHC: Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation, and restrictions of Chemicals - Substances of Very High Concern

Basic Material Management

For own produced components, often basic materials come into place. They can be added to own components and by this the chemical composition is known as the basic materials contain pure substances**. Using external systems like IMDS basic materials can be downloaded and used in SAP Product and REACH Compliance.

** Pure substances and listed substances are different objects in SAP Product Compliance. For seamless functionality these objects must be linked when existing on both sides.

Compliance Data

SAP Product and REACH Compliance uses its own objects, the compliance data objects. These CDOs are the counterparts of the products, components, supplier-material information, etc. in logistics. Using the Bill-of-Material-Transfer (BOM-Transfer) the system transfers the relevant objects for compliance to product compliance. Where required, high sophisticated logic to retrieve the best variant of a BOM (the best plant using the best usage and alternative) can be applied. These compliance data objects are used for compliance calculation and might have several versions (to still have access to historical data) 

Compliance Declarations from suppliers

Several formats are supported out of the box as "manual data maintenance", IPC1752A (this is a standard from IPC <<Association Connecting Electronics Industries>>), and MDS from IMDS.

Compliance Declarations for customer

Out of the box the system can create MDS to IMDS, IPC1752A format C, and Adobe Interactive Forms documents such as <<Declaration of Substances>> (DoS) and <<Certificate of Compliance>> (CoC).

Compliance Reporting

Here the system offers <<Core Data Services>> (CDS) views to retrieve data using compliance analysis from the system. Besides that, Excel templates are available to retrieve data from selected products or components and transfer them to Excel.

SAP Product and REACH Compliance - scope overview

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Deeper insight to BOM Transfer

As we have already touched above, the BOM Transfer copies the relevant objects from logistics to product compliance. In case the objects do not yet exist, they are created, otherwise the system updates them.

Objects

  • Materials (system will identify them and categorize them as "package", "bulk", or "product or product part"
  • BOM Structure (system uses specification database to store this information)
  • Supplier-material objects (these supplier parts are identified for most cases via source list or purchasing info record)

BOM Transfer triggers

As the figure below shows, there are certain triggers possible to initiate this transfer.

Manual transfer

Manually started by the user. That is what SAP has foreseen for trading goods. It might help during cutover, too. But for most use cases the customers want to have a higher automatization.

Bill of Material gets created or changed

That is also the SAP standard approach, however; the used workflows are not appreciated by customers and often a BOM gets changes to a relevant status before the material gets a relevant status. So, most customers rely on our own worklist that covers several use cases.

Supplier material links have changed

This is an appreciated SAP standard approach. Once the source list or purchasing info record gets created or deleted, the single material transfer is initiated by the system.

Material has changed

Not part of SAP standard functionality. But while applying the functionality of worklist from above we can handle this.

BOM Transfer - triggers

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Applying filters for BOM Transfer

To emphasize the powerfulness of BOM Transfer we shed some light to the filter criteria. Using two existing structures, for the materials, the BOM header, and the BOM positions filters might be configurated. By doing this we only get relevant data into product compliance. Further configuration might be used for BOM alternative handling. Last but not least, there are user exits available that allow us to realize special customer requirements, e.g., to not overwrite weight data in case a manual flag is set (weight data is always a challenge...)

Degrees of freedom using BOM Transfer

There exist two poles.

Pole 1 is to transfer each and every alternative into product compliance. This ensures maximal compliance; however, leads to complexity. Some use cases do not permit this approach. 

Pole 2 is to transfer only one structure to product compliance. This might be the latest changed BOM or the BOM with the smallest alternative.

Often a realistic scenario (using different plant nodes) gets implemented as the figure below shows.

BOM Transfer - degrees of freedom

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Product Compliance structure

The product compliance structure is being displayed in the system and used by the system for the compliance calculation. 

Objects in Product Compliance structure

The figure below provides an overview of (some of) the objects being used.

Product Compliance Structure - objects

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Normalizations

Normalizations provide a special view to the product compliance structure. So you might see all leaf elements, only basic materials, elements with issues only, etc. then normalizations come into the game. 

Beyond that there exists plenty of uses cases where normalizations get applied to manipulate the product compliance structure. The figure below shows one use case: the system creates for a procured component supplier parts. For these three suppliers you have instructed them to use exact basic material for steel XYZ. The suppliers won't give any compliance information to you. You plug-in the basic material to the component. Now the system considers both the basic material and the supplier parts and states "missing data". With the "prefer basic material" normalization, the supplier parts get suppressed in case there exists a basic material.

Product Compliance Structure - normalizations

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Summary

SAP Product and REACH Compliance is a powerful tool. For my customers I have created process documentations with 200+ pages, for sure this cannot be added into a blog. However, the basic concept on a high level is given.

In case of questions or required help just contact the author.

entry.title: Introducing New Safety Data Sheet Features
entry.content:
Here are the highlights for the newest features for Safety Data Sheet Management:

  • SDS Generation extended to include sections 9, 11 and 12
  • New Business Event between Partners and SAP BTP: Request Data Calculation
  • New API’s to move data from legacy systems into SAP S/4HANA for product compliance
    • GHS Classification
    • Handling and Storage
    • Safety Related Properties
  • Versioning of GHS assessments for substances
  • Compliance Requirement topic to group requirements together when based on the same compliance pattern
  • Waste Code compliance requirements versions now have aspect definitions available
  • New Aerosol Labeling app

 

In this post we will go through the system and see how to maintain and review basic data for our example industrial cleaner.

Industrial Cleaner Example

Here is the Work View, where we have an overview of the processing status of the different data under each section.

 

From the Work View we can navigate to the different sections for specific information including History and Admin Data.

Section 9 - Safety-Related Properties

We can also check our information from Section 3 - Composition / Information on Ingredients with the analytical composition.

Then we can get down to the substance level via the Manage Substances Compliance app.

 

Here we are interested in the properties and can view the Toxicological Information which has already been released.

The new Business Event on requesting data calculations enables partners to consume business events running data calculations with public API’s to read and write the dependent datasets.

 

Along with the new Compliance Requirement for the EU Waste Code, you can see here the new user interface for setting the detailed information.

For Aerosol Labeling as new app variant, under the Manage Regulatory Data app, has been developed to manage data for unpackaged products and raw materials. This accompanies a new compliance requirement for Aerosol Labeling based on the Regulatory Data Assessment compliance pattern. For more information check this out.

entry.title: Exporting and Importing Custom Objects and CPE Packages in SAP Sustainability Control Tower
entry.content:
This blog provides a broad outline of how custom objects like custom dimensions, custom measures and custom metrics can be exported and imported between different tenants, as well as the exporting and importing of CPE packages.

Restriction: Currently, we have no option to export and import the following objects.

  • KPI Tiles
  • Ambitions
  • Ambitions Groups
  • Leading and supported reporting hierarchy

Fact Sheet: Once we have defined our own set of custom dimensions, custom measures and custom metrics, we can export that set using either of the respective apps, i.e. Manage Custom Dimensions, Manage Measures, Manage Metrics. We can also export and import the created CPE (Calculation and Processing Engine) content.

To begin with, let us focus on how we can export and import custom objects. For Instance I have created a custom dimension called "Insurance Type For Employees" and updated the master data. 

Exporting and Importing Custom Objects:

Now let us choose "Go To Export" option.

Add the desired custom objects.

Select the desired custom dimension from the value help and add to the custom objects inventory.

All valid custom objects can be exported using the option "Export All".

we can find the exported file in the "Downloads" folder with the file name as "sct-custom-inventory.json" and can be renamed if required for import.

Now, let us access "Manage Custom Dimensions" app to import.

choose the file to upload,

Let us choose the option "Import All". System imports the valid custom objects.

 

Exporting and Importing CPE Packages:

Let us look at how we can export and import content from CPE packages. We can enter CPE from various possible ways. For instance I have chosen using app "CPE Process Templates for Data Management".

Let us move on to environments overview page,

from the overview page, we can select the desired package to export by choosing the option "Export".

For instance, I have chosen "SCT - Manage ESG Data", " Version 0009".

A file in the .zip file format is added to the list with the name and version of the environment. We can view the exported environment.

 

 

Now, let us import using file upload option.

After the file upload has been successful, we must choose the option "Import" and input the details.

Let us choose "Import" and check the import status.

 

 😊This brings me to the end of the "Exporting and Importing Custom Objects" walkthrough process in SAP Sustainability Control Tower. I hope this blog proves useful to the Sustainability Control Tower Team. I think this feature brings a significant value to the business during implementation process.

If you want to gain a deep understanding of  SAP Sustainability Control Tower and out of the box seamless integration with SAP EHSM, Environment Management, check out my SAP SCT & EHSM blogs mentioned below, they will give you a broad outline of SAP Sustainability Control Tower  & SAP EHSM Environment Management topics.

  1. European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) 
  2. EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-1 
  3. EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-2 
  4. EHS - Environment Management, Emission Management Part-3 
  5. SAP Sustainability Control Tower-Import Greenhouse Gas Emissions from SAP EHS Environment Management 
  6. Addendum to SAP Sustainability Control Tower-Import Greenhouse Gas Emissions from SAP EHSM-EM 
  7. SAP Sustainability Control Tower(SCT) Metric Management-Basic Custom Metric & Standard Custom Metric 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy.C

entry.title: SAP Sustainability Control Tower: ESRS S1-6 – Characteristics of the undertaking’s employees
entry.content:
Disclaimer: This blog post reflects the state of the SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) as of February 11th, 2025.

ESRS S1-6 requires a company to disclose quantitative and qualitative (/narrative) information about its employees. SAP Sustainability Control Tower (SCT) provides out of the box several quantitative metrics, hence this article focusses on how to bridge the gap for the ESRS S1-6 requirements for the quantitative data points. Specifically, the focus is on paragraph 50 a: "the undertaking shall disclose the total number of employees by head count, and breakdowns by gender and by country for countries in which the undertaking has 50 or more employees representing at least 10% of its total number of employees." 

Total Number of Employees

For the “total number of employees by head count” the SAP provided metric Total Number of Employees can be used.  

Breakdown by Gender

The breakdown by gender is currently not out of the box possible in SCT. In the additional application requirement 55 (AR 55) it is described that the company shall disclose the information in a tabular format and provides the following template:

As of now SCT doesn’t allow to display tables. However, it is possible to visualize the breakdown by gender with Standard Custom Metrics. You can create a Standard Custom Metric within the Manage Metrics app:

Then you can define the details of the Standard Custom Metric. In this case, for Z_1-6_FEMALE:

Please ensure that your leading and supported reporting hierarchies are set correctly.

I.e., if you provide information via the Measure “Workforce Headcount” and its template “Workforce Headcount – Actuals” in the Manage ESG Data app, you need to ensure that the respective hierarchies are maintained if you want them to be supported in SCT.

  • ID_COMPANY_CODE stands for Legal Entity
  • ID_ORG_UNIT stands for Reporting Line
  • ID_BUSINESS_LOC stands for Business Location

In the example above all hierarchies are provided with data and hence should be supported.

In the calculation definition you can select the Measure “Workforce Headcount”, the Calculation Method is Sum, and you select the respective filter to sum up. In this case you sum up all entries with Gender Female.  As the requirement is to disclose the headcount it is crucial to set the Value Type to Row Count.

Background: In your template you define both, MEASURE_VALUE_CHAR and MEASURE_COUNT. For the “Workforce Headcount” the former defines the full time equivalent (FTE) of each entry whereas the latter defines the headcount. In this simplified example there are in total 275 FTEs and the headcount is 350. This can be the case due to part time working employees. When defining the calculation in SCT the value type defines whether is should sum up the aggregated values (in this case the MEASURE_VALUE_CHAR – FTE) or the rows (in this case MEASURE_COUNT – headcount).

After creating the Standard Custom Metric and uploading the “Workforce Headcount – Actuals” template in the Manage ESG Data app, you can then calculate the data for the Standard Custom Metrics, and publish it. E.g., for Z_S1-6_FEMALE:

After the calculation you can already see that SCT selected the correct entries to be summed up:

In the Analyze ESG Data app you can then visualize all calculated Standard Metrics and SAP provided Metrics:

Breakdown by country for countries in which the undertaking has 50 or more employees representing at least 10% of its total number of employees.

The breakdown by country with the respective filtering of 50+ employees and representing at least 10% of the company’s total number of employees is currently not out of the box possible in SCT. In the additional application requirement 55 (AR 55) it is described that the company shall disclose the information in a tabular format and provides the following template:

As of now SCT doesn’t allow to display tables. However, it is possible to visualize the breakdown by countries with the Our Ambitions app. If you drill down the Total Number of Employees Metric under the Business Location hierarchy, you can visualize the breakdown per country. Please note that this requires the appropriate set-up of the master data and the provided actuals. Furthermore, a filtering of 50+ employees and representing at least 10% of the company’s total number of employees is also here not possible.

entry.title: The ABAP Detective and the Errant Electrolyte
entry.content:
An aspect of sustainability is minimal resource use, in both the sense of lower costs, but also the avoidance of energy use that contributes to carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuels. For an establishment on "all-electric" the latter is what might be called an externality. As a code and systems analysis detective, I look for areas of improvement.

Previous investigations into environmental monitoring tools are noted at the end; this case focuses on the efficiency and use of domestic hot water delivery. In the backwaters of the united states, standard appliances include a 30 or 40 gallon (sorry: X liters) tank with a heating source constantly attempting to maintain a set minimum temperature. Other areas use the "on-demand" approach, or hot-water-as-a-service, with a heating device close to the delivery point (let's not go into the 110 versus 220 volt infrastructure as a distraction).

To the crux of the biscuit: I set up a temperature monitor directly above the output of the hot water tank, connected to a Raspberry Pi that is polled by a central system. Other approaches would be locally collecting the data and uploading in scheduled bursts, pushing rather than pulling.

TMP102 gauge, copper, insulation. (Photo by Jim Spath)

(photo by Jim Spath)

This ad hoc wiring approach works in a non-intrusive way, as the sensor unit does not touch the copper pipes, nor contact running water. Typically throughout the day, hot water is not running, so the readings are close to ambient room temperature. When a tap is opened somewhere, preheated water goes near enough to tick up the temperature reading, then after the flows stops the temperature gradually degrades. Or, if the tank completely empties of heated water, the readings will correspondingly decline.

Standalone sensor:

TMP102 gauge with blips

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

A few times during the day, temperature rises, then declines. Not a particularly useful data set if you want to ask questions like "how hot is the hot water" or "how long did the washer run?" or even "how much did this unit of work cost?". The human brain can glance at these shapes and discern patterns, like Sherlock Holmes checking out cigar-ash-flakes in a pursuit. Writing an algorithm is a bit trickier.

The Zabbix open source suite I'm using has tools to run calculations on data feeds; for instance in some cases I'd pull values in Celsius, then add a derived parameter converting to Fahrenheit. There are ways to "look back" to prior values to determine deltas (which I've not implemented yet). You can also set up triggers to perform actions such as alerting on worst case scenarios (like overheating or frozen pipes: Ow!).

I'll gloss over the gnarly details of the Sparkfun/QWIIC monitoring hardware/software and cut to the chase: only one specific gauge per Pi, without adding multiplexing I2C complexities, so I was faced with deploying 2 Pi's with the same gauge type, or add a different gauge on 1 Pi. I chose the latter. Then the case went into what we colloquially called a "mung" state. Not "hung" but not right either.

Pi and the OS

If I could, I'd "flame" here, showing the rapid pace of software development breaks things that worked a few short years ago in surprising ways. I'm using code that someone else wrote in bash shell scripting back in 2012 or so, and that logic doesn't need to be tweaked when a new Linux kernel or even a subsystem component is written. And generally, Python examples the vendors share keeps working from year to year. Upgrades to the running environment are non-breaking other than minor steps like "oh, use Python 3.12 now instead of 3.10" that are easy enough to resolve.

My first Pi platform had a "6.1" 32-bit Linux kernel version; more recently new installs have a "6.6" kernel, where 64-bit flavors are as stable as the 32-bit. Great, why would that matter? Somewhere in the matrix, Python grew an appendage, nicknamed "venv" meaning a virtual environment. Let's add containers, crates, and virtualization while we're at it. Except this is a $10 machine with a single purpose.

TMP102 and BME280

These 2 temperature sensors are available in the chosen "ecosystem" that connects the Pi brain to the digital sensors, which could also include humidity and barometric pressure. Since I had collected and deployed at least one of each, I assumed connecting both to 1 Pi would be simple. I had code samples in shell and Python for both. "Just" follow the manufacturers instructions and "pip" it into place (no, not the old DEC PIP peripheral to peripheral connector, the new python software package installer). Failure to launch:

 

$ pip install qwiic_bme280
error: externally-managed-environment

 

Oh, darn, this didn't work, and spit out a bunch of scary messages, especially this last dooms-day-scenario:

 

You can override this, at the risk of breaking \
 your Python installation or OS,  \
 by passing --break-system-packages. \
hint: See PEP 668 for the detailed specification.

 

"Break system packages" sounds ominous. Maybe don't take that approach. 

pip breakage

(screenshot from terminal session)

A more staid detective would go read the reference manuals, consult others online, re-read the source code, then diligently make necessary repairs. Others more reactive might literally check with their vendors and ask what's up with this. Investigators with a hacking tendency would start typing likely fixes and rout/route around the damage. Command line use of a venv turned out to be fairly simple:

 

python3 -m venv venv
venv/bin/pip3 install sparkfun-qwiic-bme280

[...]
Successfully installed \
 smbus2-0.5.0 \
 sparkfun-qwiic-bme280-0.9.0 \
 sparkfun-qwiic-i2c-1.0.0

 

No doubt more experienced Python developers are aghast at the hammer approach I used.

Getting readings at the command line would now work with 2 sensors; the new one shows:

 

$ venv/bin/python3.11  /path/to/sparkfun.py  temperature_celsius
16.2118

 

Fine, a 3-pipe problem rather than a trivial 2-pipe one. Except the whole intent is to automate sensor data collection, not have to run a command line manually. I'll leave out the messy trial-and-error details, other than to share that the Zabbix process debug steps boil down to seeing whether a parameter shows as "not supported" or returns an error message such as "got string, expecting a number" in the console, and checking the agent logs for clues (like: "The Qwiic BME280 device isn't connected to the system. Please check your connection" when I used the wrong sensor).

Stuffed inside an old shell script are new links to the monitor logic.

 

/path/venv/python3 /path/sparkfun-venv.py temperature_celsius

 

The middle section didn't need to have any virtual environment goo but for the sake of my future self looking at these sensors after the next incompatibility will possibly recall why.

Before going to the trouble of writing calculation or trigger steps in parameter definitions I used a handy UI feature in Zabbix that overlays 2 measurements in one chart. First, make sure both metrics are stable:

2 charts

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

Then, add the second parameter to a dashboard widget. I didn't muck around with colors, lines, or transparencies, taking the defaults as good enough for the quick quality control / QC check.

2 temperature sensors on 1 chart

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

Next problems to solve?

  • The 2 gauges differ under "no flow" states by over 1 degree.
  • Alerts/triggers
  • Validation/calibration

Calibration

Back in the last century, my polytechnic/polymath education included calibrating thermometers by determining how close their readings were to freezing boiling water temperatures. A memorable lesson in theory versus practice, tossing aside the novice assumption that a device always shows accurate values. How many degrees or tenths-of-degrees plus or minus the correct reading? (let's skip over side issues such as does water boil at exactly 100 degrees Celsius at any specific elevation or location).

What I'd probably investigate next is recording the temperature at the taps compared to the utility room infrastructure, particularly after hearing anecdotal comments such as "the kitchen sink faucet is not hot enough."

NOTES / REFERENCES

Shell:

  • https://znil.net/index.php/Zabbix:Template_Raspberry_Pi
  • https://znil.net/index.php?title=Temperatur_/_Spannung_etc._des_Raspberry_Pi_selbst_auslesen

Python:

  • http://rptl.io/venv
  • https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html
  • https://peps.python.org/pep-0668/
  • https://github.com/sparkfun/qwiic_bme280_py

SAP Community posts:

  • The ABAP Detective Takes The Summer Heat
  • The ABAP Detective Gets Their Clock Cleaned
  • The ABAP Detective Takes The Heat, or How To Cleanse Data

Also:

  • Intra home detectors

entry.title: Unlocking Scope 3 Transparency with SAP Sustainability Data Exchange - Deep Dive & Demo
entry.content:
SAP Sustainability Data Exchange (SDX) provides a seamless, automated solution for exchanging product carbon footprint (PCF) data, ensuring compliance with global standards and enabling data-driven sustainability decisions.

If you’re a sustainability lead, supply chain professional, or technical expert working on emissions tracking, this deep dive session is essential viewing. It offers a comprehensive walkthrough of SDX’s capabilities and how businesses can implement it to scale sustainability reporting.

Key Benefits of SAP Sustainability Data Exchange

SAP SDX is designed to facilitate efficient, accurate, and standardized PCF data sharing across the value chain. Here's how:

1. Standardized, Interoperable Data Exchange

  • Built to support industry standards like WBCSD PACT and Catena-X, ensuring data compatibility across networks.
  • Maintains data sovereignty, allowing companies to control their shared data.

2. Efficient Supplier Data Requests

  • Buyers can request supplier-specific PCF data for purchased products.
  • Bulk request capabilities streamline data collection from multiple partners at once.

3. Centralized PCF Inventory & Audit Readiness

  • Consolidated view of all purchased and finished product footprints.
  • Ensures compliance with ESG regulations and simplifies audit processes.

For a more detailed technical breakdown, explore the official Deep Dive PDF.

Why You Should Watch the Deep Dive Session

Understanding the full potential of SAP SDX is crucial for businesses looking to:


✔ Move from estimated to actual emissions data and improve reporting accuracy.
✔ Ensure compliance with evolving ESG regulations.
✔ Identify CO₂ reduction hotspots and make informed decisions.
✔ Automate emissions tracking across complex supply chains.

This on-demand deep dive video will walk you through real use cases, live demos, and best practices for implementing SDX in your sustainability strategy. Watch it here.

Take the Next Step – Try SAP SDX for Free

To experience SDX firsthand, SAP offers a free 30-day trial where you can explore its functionalities with sample data and guided processes. This is your opportunity to see how SDX can help your business enhance supply chain transparency and streamline carbon reporting.

➡ Start your free trial now!

Have questions or looking for implementation guidance? Reach out to us—we’re here to help you drive sustainability forward. 🚀

Curious and want to learn more?

Visit SAP.com/SDX  for more exciting product information. We are now supporting digital sales experience! You can also find out more detailed guides on Help Portal and the future capabilities on the Roadmap Explorer.

Partners can also access the latest assets on the Partner Portal.

See how our reference customer WITTE Automotive talk about their decarbonization journey with SAP Sustainability solutions. 

**************************

Jessica Bartels, Product Marketing Lead - SAP Sustainability Data Exchange

#sapsustainabilitydataexchange #sapfootprintmanagement #sapsustainabilitycontroltower #sapbusinessnetwork
